text: The surgeon uses a prograsp instrument in the second arm from the patient's right to lift tissue, while maneuvering the arm to avoid obstruction. A fenestrated bipolar instrument is utilized from the far lateral port, and scissors are introduced through the subcyphoid working port, facilitating the laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Wristed instruments are employed for precise tissue division, achieving a clear critical view of safety without the need for ICG green dye.

The surgeon identifies a bridging vein beneath the gallbladder and cuts it, creating a wide triangle in the closed triangle area. The gallbladder is initially pushed against the liver bed, but the surgeon adjusts the top arm to pull the gallbladder away, aiming to expose the cystic plate.

The surgeon uses a wristed instrument to grasp the underside of the gallbladder, pulling it away to expose more of the cystic plate and achieve a clear critical view of safety. The falciform ligament is maneuvered towards the belly button to facilitate instrument passage. Medium hemoclips are applied to the end artery, followed by cutting the cystic duct with scissors.
The surgeon uses scissors to cauterize along the length of the artery, demonstrating precise control with robotic wristed instruments.

The surgeon navigates the intra-hepatic gallbladder using wristed instruments, allowing precise manipulation of the gallbladder and liver bed despite complex angles. The robotic approach enhances precision in burning and dissection compared to traditional laparoscopic techniques.
